sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient, Betty, presents with a history of asthma that has been frustrating her with frequent exacerbations, particularly when she gets sick with a cold.
She reports using her albuterol inhaler daily and sometimes at night, with some relief but still experiencing symptoms.
She has had multiple urgent care visits and one emergency department visit in the past year, which have resulted in the use of prednisone.
She also notes that exposure to her aunt's cat triggers her asthma symptoms.
She has not had any hospitalizations due to asthma in the past 20 years.
She denies smoking, vaping, or marijuana use, but occasionally goes to bars where she is exposed to secondhand smoke.
Past medical history: Not mentioned.
Review of systems: Not mentioned.
Current medications: Albuterol inhaler 2 puffs every 4-6 hours as needed.
Objective:
Vital signs: Blood pressure 128/82, respiratory rate 16, oxygen saturation 99% on room air.

Physical exam: Diminished lung sounds throughout with occasional slight expiratory wheeze bilaterally, heart regular rate and rhythm without ectopic beats or murmurs, neck is supple with midline trachea, no lymphadenopathy, no carotid bruit, strong bilateral pulses, no clubbing on fingertips, and brisk capillary refill.
Test Results: Pulmonary function test results consistent with asthma.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient's symptoms are consistent with moderate intermittent asthma.
Problem list: Asthma; Asthma (moderate intermittent); Asthma (status: active); Asthma (status: confirmed)
Plan:
I prescribed Flovent 110 micrograms, one puff twice per day to prevent daily symptoms and reduce reliance on albuterol inhaler.
The patient can continue using albuterol inhaler 2 puffs every 4-6 hours as needed.
I will put together an asthma action plan to help manage symptoms and identify triggers.
Follow-up: I would like to see the patient in a month or sooner if symptoms worsen.
